title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Century Egg Congee Recipe: Authentic Chinese Breakfast
seo.metaDescription: Master authentic Century Egg Congee with our easy-to-follow recipe. Learn how to make this comforting Chinese breakfast dish at home. Perfect for a savory start!
longTailKeywords: how to make Century Egg Congee
Century Egg Congee step by step
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy Century Egg Congee garnished with green onions and a sliced century egg.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Century Egg Congee, a classic Chinese breakfast.

description: This classic Chinese breakfast offers a comforting bowl of silky smooth rice porridge, perfectly complemented by the unique savory depth of century eggs. Often enjoyed with shredded pork or other toppings, it is a nourishing and flavorful start to any day. Its mild yet complex flavors make it a beloved staple in Chinese homes.

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the jasmine rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. Drain well. For extra creamy congee, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes before cooking, then drain.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring it to a rolling boil over high heat, then immediately reduce the heat to low. Cover the pot, leaving a small gap for steam to escape, and simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Pork
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, peel the century eggs and dice them into small pieces. If using pork, thinly slice or shred it. Marinate the pork with a pinch of salt, white pepper, 1 tablespoon of Shaoxing wine (if using), and 1 teaspoon of cornstarch (if using) for 10 minutes. Thinly slice the ginger and chop the green onions.

instructions.title: Add Pork and Century Eggs
instructions.text: After 30 minutes of simmering, the rice should have broken down and the congee will be thickening. Add the marinated pork to the congee, stirring gently to separate the pieces. Cook for 5 minutes, then add most of the diced century eggs (reserve some for garnish if desired) and julienned ginger.

instructions.title: Season and Finish Cooking
instructions.text: Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes, or until the congee reaches your desired consistency and the pork is fully cooked. If the congee becomes too thick, add more hot water or broth, a little at a time, until it is just right. Season generously with salt and white pepper to taste.

instructions.title: Serve Hot
instructions.text: Ladle the hot century egg congee into individual bowls. Garnish each serving with the remaining diced century egg, chopped green onions, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Serve immediately and enjoy this comforting Chinese breakfast.

tips: For extra creamy congee, freeze the washed rice for a few hours before cooking. The frozen rice grains will break down faster and create a smoother texture.
If you find the strong ammonia smell of century eggs off-putting, you can briefly blanch the diced eggs in boiling water for 1-2 minutes to mellow their flavor before adding them to the congee.
Congee tends to thicken as it cools. If you have leftovers, reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave, adding a splash of water or broth to reach your preferred consistency.
article:
The comforting embrace of a warm bowl of congee is a quintessential experience in Chinese cuisine, especially at breakfast. This savory rice porridge, known as "jook" in Cantonese, is a staple that soothes the stomach and warms the soul. While congee comes in countless variations, one of the most iconic and beloved is Congee with Century Egg and Pork. This dish offers a unique blend of textures and flavors, making it a must-try for anyone exploring authentic Chinese breakfast recipes. It’s a dish that embodies both simplicity and profound taste, a testament to the culinary ingenuity of Chinese home cooking.

## What is Century Egg Congee?

Century Egg Congee, often featuring tender pork (皮蛋瘦肉粥, pídàn shòuròu zhōu), is a deeply satisfying rice porridge characterized by its silky-smooth texture and complex flavors. At its heart is the congee itself – rice cooked in a large volume of water or broth until the grains have completely broken down, creating a thick, creamy gruel. The star ingredient, the century egg (also known as a preserved egg or hundred-year egg), is a preserved duck, chicken, or quail egg that has undergone a curing process in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls for several weeks or months. This process transforms the egg white into a translucent, dark jelly with a unique, slightly pungent aroma and a savory, umami-rich flavor. The yolk becomes a creamy, dark green or grey paste. When combined with the mildness of the congee and the savory notes of shredded pork, the century egg adds an earthy depth and a striking visual contrast that elevates the dish from simple porridge to an unforgettable culinary experience. The Century Egg Congee is not just food; it's a cultural icon, cherished for its nourishing qualities and distinctive taste.

## Ingredients for Century Egg Congee

Making authentic Century Egg Congee requires a few key components, all readily available in most Asian grocery stores. The quality of your ingredients will directly influence the final taste and texture of your congee.

*   **Rice:** 1 cup Jasmine rice or short-grain rice. Jasmine rice gives a fragrant and slightly sticky texture, while short-grain rice breaks down beautifully for a creamy consistency.
*   **Water/Broth:** 8-10 cups cold water or chicken broth. Broth will add a richer depth of flavor.
*   **Pork:** 8 oz lean pork loin or tenderloin, shredded or diced. Marinating the pork beforehand enhances its tenderness and flavor.
*   **Century Eggs:** 2-3 century eggs, peeled and diced into small cubes or wedges.
*   **Ginger:** 1-inch piece of fresh ginger, thinly sliced or julienned. Ginger helps to balance the century egg’s distinct aroma.
*   **Scallions:** 2-3 stalks, chopped for garnish.
*   **Seasoning:** Salt to taste, a dash of white pepper.
*   **Pork Marinade (Optional but Recommended):** 1 tsp soy sauce, 1 tsp sesame oil, 1 tsp cornstarch, a pinch of white pepper.

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Follow these steps to create your own delicious Century Egg Congee at home. Patience is key to achieving that perfect silky texture.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Wash the rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. This removes excess starch, preventing the congee from becoming gummy. For a quicker cook time and creamier texture, you can soak the rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even freeze it overnight (the freezing and thawing process creates fissures in the rice grains, allowing them to break down faster).
2.  **Marinate the Pork:** If using, shred or dice the pork. In a small bowl, combine the pork with soy sauce, sesame oil, cornstarch, and white pepper. Mix well and let it marinate for at least 15 minutes while you prepare other ingredients.
3.  **Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger:** Peel the century eggs carefully. Rinse them under cold water to remove any lingering residue from the preservation process, then dice them into small cubes or wedges. Peel and thinly slice or julienne the fresh ginger.
4.  **Start the Congee Base:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the washed rice and cold water or broth. Bring it to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom.
5.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to very low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer for at least 1.5 to 2 hours. Stir every 15-20 minutes, especially towards the end, to prevent sticking and ensure even cooking. The rice grains should break down completely, forming a thick, creamy porridge. If the congee becomes too thick, add more hot water or broth to reach your desired consistency.
6.  **Add Pork and Century Eggs:** When the congee has reached your desired texture, increase the heat slightly. Add the marinated pork to the pot, stirring constantly for 2-3 minutes until the pork is cooked through and tender. Then, stir in most of the diced century eggs (reserve some for garnish).
7.  **Season and Serve:** Season the Century Egg Congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Stir well. Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Garnish generously with fresh chopped scallions and the reserved century egg pieces. A drizzle of sesame oil is also a popular finishing touch.

## Tips for Perfect Congee

Achieving the perfect bowl of Century Egg Congee involves more than just throwing ingredients into a pot. Here are some tips to elevate your congee game:

*   **Rice to Water Ratio:** A good starting point is 1 cup of rice to 8-10 cups of liquid. Adjust based on how thick or thin you prefer your congee.
*   **Patience is a Virtue:** The best congee is cooked low and slow. This allows the rice grains to fully break down and release their starch, resulting in that signature creamy texture. Don't rush the process.
*   **Stirring:** While you don't need to stir constantly, periodic stirring is crucial, especially when the congee starts to thicken, to prevent it from sticking to the bottom of the pot and burning.
*   **Broth Over Water:** Using chicken or pork broth instead of plain water will significantly enhance the flavor profile of your congee, giving it a richer, more savory base.
*   **Pre-soaking or Freezing Rice:** As mentioned, pre-soaking rice for at least 30 minutes or freezing it overnight can significantly reduce cooking time and aid in achieving a creamier texture.
*   **Don't Skimp on Ginger:** Fresh ginger is vital in Century Egg Congee. Its bright, peppery notes cut through the richness of the pork and complement the century egg's unique flavor, creating a harmonious balance.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Serving Century Egg Congee well enhances its flavors and presentation, making it an even more delightful breakfast or light meal.

*   **Garnishes are Key:** Freshly chopped scallions are non-negotiable, adding a burst of color and a mild oniony crunch. A few drops of sesame oil swirled in just before serving adds a nutty aroma. Some enthusiasts also enjoy a sprinkle of white pepper, fried shallots, or fresh cilantro.
*   **Classic Pairings:** The ultimate companion to Century Egg Congee is Youtiao (油条), Chinese fried dough sticks. These crispy, savory sticks are perfect for dipping into the hot congee, soaking up all the delicious flavors.
*   **Side Dishes:** Congee often pairs wonderfully with a variety of pickled vegetables, such as pickled radish (zai choy), fermented bean curd, or spicy pickled mustard greens. These provide a tangy contrast to the congee's richness. A small dish of soy sauce, chili oil, or a light vinegar can also be offered on the side for those who wish to customize their flavor.
*   **Making it a Feast:** While Century Egg Congee is substantial enough on its own, it can also be part of a larger Chinese breakfast spread alongside dim sum items, steamed buns, or other stir-fried noodles.

## Variations & Add-ins

The beauty of congee lies in its versatility. While Century Egg Congee is a classic, you can easily customize it with various ingredients to suit your taste or what you have on hand.

*   **Other Meats:** Instead of pork, consider shredded chicken (often called "chicken congee" or "ji rou zhou"), salted pork, ground pork, or even thinly sliced fish (fish congee or "yu pian zhou"). Each adds a distinct flavor profile.
*   **Vegetables:** For added nutrients and texture, stir in finely diced carrots, mushrooms (shiitake or oyster), corn kernels, or wilted spinach towards the end of cooking.
*   **Flavor Boosters:** Dried scallops, rehydrated and shredded, add an incredible depth of umami. A handful of roasted peanuts can provide a pleasant crunch. Goji berries can also be added for a touch of sweetness and color.
*   **Other Eggs:** While the century egg is the star, you can also add slices of hard-boiled regular eggs or even salted duck egg (咸蛋, xiándàn) for another layer of savory richness. Some recipes combine century egg and salted duck egg for a "double egg congee."
*   **Spices:** A tiny pinch of five-spice powder or a star anise pod can be added to the congee during the simmering process for a more aromatic broth, though this is less common for the classic Century Egg Congee.

Whether you're new to Chinese cuisine or a seasoned enthusiast, mastering Century Egg Congee is a rewarding culinary journey. Its unique blend of comfort, flavor, and texture makes it a truly special dish that embodies the heart of Chinese breakfast traditions. Give it a try, and you might just discover your new favorite breakfast staple.
